unitTitle: Unit 1: Present simple, present continuous & stative verbs
unitSubtitle: Habits, actions happening now, and verbs that usually avoid continuous forms

rows.label: Negative
rows.left: I / you / we / they don't play

rows.label: Question
rows.left: Do I / you / we / they play?

sections.title: Examples
items: Tom catches the bus every morning.
My aunt lives in a small town.

sections.title: Remember
items: Frequency adverbs usually come before the main verb.
They come after the verb be.

rows.label: Positive
rows.right: he / she / it is playing; you / we / they are playing

rows.label: Negative
rows.right: he / she / it isn't playing; you / we / they aren't playing

rows.label: Question
rows.right: Is he / she / it playing? Are you / we / they playing?

sections.title: Examples
items: Sam is talking on the phone right now.
Lucy is staying with her grandparents this week.
My sister is always leaving her clothes on the floor!

sections.title: What are stative verbs?
sections.description: Stative verbs describe states, feelings, opinions, senses or possession. We do not normally use them in continuous forms.

sections.title: Compare
incorrectExamples: I am understanding what you mean.

sections.title: Be careful
sections.description: Some verbs can be stative or active depending on the meaning.
examples: What do you think of this song? = opinion
I'm thinking about changing schools. = considering

formatDefinitions.displayName: Build the sentence with visual tiles
formatDefinitions.pedagogicalGoal: The learner answers a meaningful visual/context question by building the sentence, instead of translating a slash prompt.

formatDefinitions.displayName: Choose the verb tile and conjugate it

interactiveFormatSystem.goal: Reusable interactive exercise screens for grammar practice.

screenTypes.purpose: Test-style choice: choose the correct conjugated form.
Write only the missing conjugated form inside a sentence gap.
See a full sentence with the wrong conjugation marked and type the replacement.
Choose a base verb from word tiles, then write it correctly conjugated in the gap.
Find wrong conjugations in a text one at a time, then write each correction.
studentFlow: Tap a wrong verb phrase and press Check
Press Continue to confirm the mark and advance the progress bar
Write the correction for that error
Press Continue to see the fix in green, then find the next error

screenTypes.purpose: Build a sentence by ordering word tiles, with extra distractors.

customScreens.explanation: Here, think describes a mental action, so the continuous form is possible.

hintPrompts: Is this a habit or something happening now?
Do you need am, is or are?

exercises.title: A: Build the routine
exercises.instructions: Look at the picture and answer the question by building the sentence. Some words are extra.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap words to add them to the end, or drag them to place them anywhere.

items.answer: Every morning, Jake walks to school.
acceptedAnswers: Every morning, Jake walks to school.
Jake walks to school every morning.
items.explanation: Use the present simple for routines. Jake is a singular subject, so walk becomes walks.
items.legacyDisplayPrompt: Jake / every morning / walk to school

topPrompt.contextQuestion: What does Jake do every morning?

items.contextQuestion: What does Jake do every morning?

visualPrompt.altText: Jake walks to school in the morning.
visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Jake walking to school in the morning, with a warm sunrise and a school building in the background. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: soft morning light. Composition: medium-wide shot, clear action, no visible text.

items.answer: Mia usually drinks tea for breakfast.
acceptedAnswers: Mia usually drinks tea for breakfast.
items.explanation: Usually goes before the main verb. Mia is singular, so drink becomes drinks.
items.legacyDisplayPrompt: Mia / usually / drink tea for breakfast

topPrompt.contextQuestion: What does Mia usually drink for breakfast?

items.contextQuestion: What does Mia usually drink for breakfast?

visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Mia sitting at a breakfast table and drinking tea, with toast and a simple kitchen background. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: natural morning light. Composition: medium shot, clear action, no visible text.

prompt.subject: Sara
prompt.verbPhrase: do her homework in the library

items.answer: After school, Sara often does her homework in the library.
acceptedAnswers: After school, Sara often does her homework in the library.
Sara often does her homework in the library after school.
items.explanation: Often normally goes before the main verb. With Sara, use does, not do.
items.legacyDisplayPrompt: Sara / after school / often / do her homework in the library

topPrompt.contextQuestion: What does Sara often do after school?

items.contextQuestion: What does Sara often do after school?

visualPrompt.altText: Sara does her homework in the library after school.
visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Sara doing homework at a library table after school, with bookshelves and a backpack nearby. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: calm indoor afternoon light. Composition: medium shot, clear action, no visible text.

items.answer: Lena visits her grandparents twice a month.
acceptedAnswers: Lena visits her grandparents twice a month.
Twice a month, Lena visits her grandparents.
items.explanation: Twice a month shows a repeated habit. Lena is singular, so visit becomes visits.
items.legacyDisplayPrompt: Lena / visit her grandparents / twice a month

topPrompt.contextQuestion: Who does Lena visit twice a month?

items.contextQuestion: Who does Lena visit twice a month?

visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Lena visiting her grandparents at their home, smiling together near the front door. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: warm natural light. Composition: medium shot, clear relationship, no visible text.

items.answer: Emma never stays up late on weekdays.
acceptedAnswers: Emma never stays up late on weekdays.
items.explanation: Never goes before the main verb. Emma is singular, so stay becomes stays.
items.legacyDisplayPrompt: Emma / never / stay up late / on weekdays

topPrompt.contextQuestion: What does Emma never do on weekdays?

items.contextQuestion: What does Emma never do on weekdays?

visualPrompt.altText: Emma goes to bed on time on a weekday night.
visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Emma in her bedroom on a weekday night, looking sleepy and getting ready for bed early, with a small bedside lamp and school bag nearby. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: soft evening light. Composition: medium shot, clear routine, no visible text.

items.explanation: Every Saturday describes a routine. Nora is singular, so play becomes plays.

topPrompt.contextQuestion: What does Nora do every Saturday?

items.contextQuestion: What does Nora do every Saturday?

visualPrompt.imageGenerationPrompt: Nora playing basketball on an outdoor court on a sunny Saturday, dribbling the ball with a hoop in the background. Style: bright flat vector illustration for a language-learning app. Lighting: natural daylight. Composition: medium action shot, sharp focus, no visible text.

visualSupport.purpose: associate meaning with image and sentence, not translation

contextQuestion.purpose: make the learner answer a real context question with tiles

exercises.title: B: What is happening now?
exercises.instructions: Write the correct present continuous form in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Type only the missing conjugated form.

items.sentence: Be quiet! The baby ...... .
items.explanation: Use the present continuous for something happening now. The baby is singular, so use is sleeping.
items.template: Be quiet! The baby {gap1} .

items.sentence: We ...... the film because it's boring.
items.explanation: Use the negative present continuous: subject + am/is/are + not + verb-ing. With we, use aren't watching.
items.template: We {gap1} the film because it's boring.
items.blankSentence: We ____ the film because it's boring.

items.sentence: At the moment, Carla ...... for her science test.
items.explanation: At the moment tells us the action is happening now, so use is studying.
items.template: At the moment, Carla {gap1} for her science test.
items.blankSentence: At the moment, Carla ____ for her science test.

items.sentence: Tom ...... with his cousins this week.
items.explanation: This week describes a temporary situation, so the present continuous is natural.
items.template: Tom {gap1} with his cousins this week.
items.blankSentence: Tom ____ with his cousins this week.

items.sentence: I ...... ! I'm serious!
items.explanation: Use am not with I. The speaker is talking about what they are doing right now.
items.template: I {gap1} ! I'm serious!

items.sentence: My brother ...... my headphones without asking!
items.explanation: Always with the present continuous can show annoyance about a repeated action.
items.template: My brother {gap1} my headphones without asking!
items.blankSentence: My brother ____ my headphones without asking!

items.sentence: We ...... dinner right now. Can I call you later?
items.explanation: Right now shows the action is happening at this moment. With we, use are eating.
items.template: We {gap1} dinner right now. Can I call you later?
items.blankSentence: We ____ dinner right now. Can I call you later?

items.sentence: ...... the computer at the moment?
items.explanation: In present continuous questions, put are before you: Are you using...?
items.template: {gap1} the computer at the moment?

exercises.title: C: Correct the tense
exercises.instructions: Read the full sentence. The wrong conjugation is marked. Type the correct replacement only.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write only the correction for the marked part.

items.sentence: **Are students needing** a lot of sleep during exams?
items.explanation: Need is normally a stative verb, so we usually use the present simple: Do students need...?
items.incorrectSentence: **Are students needing** a lot of sleep during exams?
items.fullCorrectSentence: Do students need a lot of sleep during exams?
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: Do students need a lot of sleep during exams?

items.sentence: Why are you so quiet? I hope you **don't feel** upset.
items.explanation: The speaker is talking about how someone seems right now, so the present continuous sounds natural.
items.incorrectSentence: Why are you so quiet? I hope you **don't feel** upset.
items.fullCorrectSentence: Why are you so quiet? I hope you aren't feeling upset.
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: Why are you so quiet? I hope you aren't feeling upset.
Why are you so quiet? I hope you are not feeling upset.

items.sentence: My dad **is working** in a bank in the city centre.
items.explanation: A permanent or long-term job is usually expressed with the present simple.
items.incorrectSentence: My dad **is working** in a bank in the city centre.
items.fullCorrectSentence: My dad works in a bank in the city centre.
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: My dad works in a bank in the city centre.

items.sentence: **Does Anna read** that magazine right now?
items.explanation: Right now signals the present continuous. In a question, use Is Anna reading...?
items.incorrectSentence: **Does Anna read** that magazine right now?
items.fullCorrectSentence: Is Anna reading that magazine right now?
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: Is Anna reading that magazine right now?

items.sentence: I **am always taking** the bus to college.
items.explanation: This sentence describes a normal routine, so use the present simple.
items.incorrectSentence: I **am always taking** the bus to college.
items.fullCorrectSentence: I always take the bus to college.
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: I always take the bus to college.

items.sentence: This game console **is working** with two batteries.
items.explanation: This describes how the console functions in general, so the present simple is correct.
items.incorrectSentence: This game console **is working** with two batteries.
items.fullCorrectSentence: This game console works with two batteries.
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: This game console works with two batteries.

items.sentence: Look at the score! **Does our team play** well today?
items.explanation: The score is happening now, so the present continuous is more natural.
items.incorrectSentence: Look at the score! **Does our team play** well today?
items.fullCorrectSentence: Look at the score! Is our team playing well today?
fullSentenceAcceptedAnswers: Look at the score! Is our team playing well today?

items.sentence: Most teenagers **are liking** music festivals.
items.explanation: Like is normally stative when it expresses preference, so use the present simple.

exercises.title: D: Choose the natural form
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct conjugated form for the sentence.

items.completedSentence: I am staying with my uncle this month.
items.explanation: This month describes a temporary situation, so the present continuous is the best choice.
items.template: I {choice} with my uncle this month.

items.completedSentence: We don't eat meat very often.
items.explanation: Very often describes a general habit, so use the present simple.
items.template: We {choice} meat very often.

items.completedSentence: Olivia is doing her homework right now.
items.explanation: Right now signals the present continuous.
items.template: Olivia {choice} her homework right now.

items.completedSentence: Does Ben usually drive to work?
items.explanation: Usually describes a routine, so use the present simple question form.
items.template: {choice} to work?

items.completedSentence: In tennis, you hit the ball over the net.
items.explanation: This is a general fact about tennis, so use the present simple.
items.template: In tennis, you {choice} the ball over the net.

items.completedSentence: I check my emails every evening.
items.explanation: Every evening describes a routine, so use the present simple.
items.template: I {choice} my emails every evening.

items.sentenceAfter: for the exam at the moment?
items.completedSentence: Are they studying for the exam at the moment?
items.explanation: At the moment signals the present continuous question form.
items.template: {choice} for the exam at the moment?

items.completedSentence: Maria and Ellie are becoming more confident these days.
items.explanation: These days can describe a changing temporary situation, so the present continuous is natural.
items.template: Maria and Ellie {choice} more confident these days.

items.completedSentence: A good teacher understands their students.
items.explanation: Understand is normally stative, and this sentence expresses a general truth.
items.template: A good teacher {choice} their students.

items.completedSentence: How do you pronounce this word?
items.explanation: This asks about the general correct pronunciation of a word, so the present simple is natural.
items.template: How {choice} this word?

exercises.exerciseTypeName: Choose the Verb Tile and Conjugate It
exercises.title: E: Choose the verb, make the form
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct base verb from the tiles. Then write its correct present simple or present continuous form in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap a verb tile, then type the conjugated form.
exercises.grammarFocus: present simple, present continuous and stative verbs

items.sentence: Tourists usually ...... in this hotel during the summer.
items.completedSentence: Tourists usually stay in this hotel during the summer.
items.explanation: Usually describes a general habit. Tourists is plural, so use stay.
items.template: Tourists usually {gap1} in this hotel during the summer.
items.blankSentence: Tourists usually ____ in this hotel during the summer.

items.sentence: ...... you ...... to the radio, or can I switch it off?
items.completedSentence: Are you listening to the radio, or can I switch it off?
items.explanation: The speaker is asking about an action happening now, so use the present continuous question form.
items.template: {gap1} you {gap2} to the radio, or can I switch it off?
items.blankSentence: ____ you ____ to the radio, or can I switch it off?

items.sentence: Regular exercise ...... you feel healthier.
items.completedSentence: Regular exercise helps you feel healthier.
items.explanation: This is a general fact. Regular exercise is singular, so help becomes helps.
items.template: Regular exercise {gap1} you feel healthier.

items.sentence: I ...... my sister's coat today because mine is dirty.
items.completedSentence: I am wearing my sister's coat today because mine is dirty.
items.explanation: Today describes a temporary situation, so use the present continuous.
items.template: I {gap1} my sister's coat today because mine is dirty.
items.blankSentence: I ____ my sister's coat today because mine is dirty.

items.sentence: ...... Emma always ...... the shopping on Fridays?
items.completedSentence: Does Emma always do the shopping on Fridays?
items.explanation: Always and on Fridays describe a routine. In present simple questions with a singular subject, use Does + base verb.
items.template: {gap1} Emma always {gap2} the shopping on Fridays?
items.blankSentence: ____ Emma always ____ the shopping on Fridays?

items.sentence: ...... you ...... for your keys? They're on the table.
items.completedSentence: Are you looking for your keys? They're on the table.
items.explanation: The speaker is asking about an action happening now, so use Are you looking...?
items.template: {gap1} you {gap2} for your keys? They're on the table.
items.blankSentence: ____ you ____ for your keys? They're on the table.

items.sentence: You ...... that box correctly. It's going to fall.
items.completedSentence: You aren't carrying that box correctly. It's going to fall.
items.explanation: The action is happening now, and the sentence is negative, so use aren't carrying.
items.template: You {gap1} that box correctly. It's going to fall.
items.blankSentence: You ____ that box correctly. It's going to fall.

items.sentence: This backpack ...... to my brother.
items.completedSentence: This backpack belongs to my brother.
items.explanation: Belong is normally stative, so use the present simple. Backpack is singular, so belong becomes belongs.
items.template: This backpack {gap1} to my brother.

exercises.title: F: Correct the wrong tenses
exercises.instructions: Mark one wrongly conjugated verb phrase at a time. After each correct mark, write its correction before finding the next error.
exercises.studentInstruction: Find an error, confirm it, then write its correction. Repeat until all 10 are fixed.
exercises.grammarFocus: present simple, present continuous and stative verbs in context
exercises.passage: One hobby I am enjoying a lot is painting. First, you are choosing the colours you want to use. It is looking easy at first, but good painting takes practice. You are needing patience and creativity. When people make mistakes, they sometimes are throwing their work away and starting again. You are improving by practising regularly. Some people are preferring photography, but I am not believing it's more interesting than painting. Right now, I paint a picture for my bedroom. My sister practises the guitar in the next room, so it's quite noisy!

items.explanation: Enjoy describes a general feeling here, so the present simple is better.
This sentence gives a general instruction, not an action happening now.
Look means seem here, so it is stative. Use the present simple.
Need is normally stative, so use the present simple.
Sometimes describes a general habit, so use the present simple.
This is a general statement about how people get better, so use the present simple.
Prefer is normally stative, so use the present simple.
Believe is normally stative, so use the present simple negative.
Right now signals an action happening at this moment, so use the present continuous.
The sister is playing in the next room now, so the present continuous is the natural form.

contentBanks.implementationNote: Original exercise items used as content bank for practice screen generation.

implementationNotes: Word-order screens use visual/context prompts instead of slash-separated text prompts. The UI should render visualPrompt and contextQuestion as the top prompt; legacyDisplayPrompt is kept only for authoring/debugging.
